sentences.chinese: 唐曰：「主臣！
sentences.literal: Tang said, "Lord—your minister!"
sentences.idiomatic: Tang said, "Your Majesty, your servant spoke out of turn—I am awestruck at my own presumption!"

sentences.chinese: 陛下雖有廉頗、李牧，不能用也。」
sentences.literal: "Though Your Majesty had Lian Po and Li Mu, you could not employ them."
sentences.idiomatic: "Even with men like Lian Po and Li Mu in the realm, Your Majesty could not actually use them."

sentences.chinese: 上怒，起入禁中。
sentences.literal: The emperor grew angry, rose, and went into the inner apartments.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor flared up, stood, and withdrew to the private quarters.

sentences.chinese: 良久，召唐讓曰：「公眾辱我，獨亡間處乎？」
sentences.literal: After a long while he summoned Tang and rebuked him: "You have publicly humiliated me—have you no place where we could have spoken alone?"
sentences.idiomatic: When he finally called Feng Tang back, he scolded him: "Why shame me in front of everyone? Could we not have had this conversation in private?"

sentences.chinese: 唐謝曰：「鄙人不知忌諱。」
sentences.literal: Tang apologized, saying, "This rustic did not know what was forbidden."
sentences.idiomatic: Feng Tang bowed: "I am a blunt countryman; I did not know the taboos."

sentences.chinese: 當是時，匈奴新大入朝那，殺北地都尉卬。
sentences.literal: At that time the Xiongnu had recently made a major incursion into Chaona, killing Ang, commandant of Beidi.
sentences.idiomatic: Just then the Xiongnu had struck deep into Chaona and slain Sun Ang, the commandant of Beidi.

sentences.chinese: 上以胡寇為意，乃卒復問唐曰：「公何以言吾不能用頗、牧也？」
sentences.literal: The sovereign had the Hu raiders on his mind and therefore finally asked Tang again, "By what do you say I could not employ Po and Mu?"
sentences.idiomatic: With the barbarian threat weighing on him, the emperor circled back: "What did you mean when you said I could not use Lian Po and Li Mu even if I had them?"

sentences.chinese: 唐對曰，「臣聞上古王者遣將也，跪而推轂，曰：『□以內寡人制之，□以外將軍制之；
sentences.literal: Tang replied, "I have heard that when the high kings of antiquity dispatched a general they knelt and pushed the chariot hub, saying, 'Within the [gate] I control it; outside the [gate] the general controls it;
sentences.idiomatic: Feng Tang answered, "The old stories say that when a true king sent out an army he knelt to push the wheels and told the commander, 'What lies inside the capital is mine; what lies beyond the passes is yours;

sentences.chinese: 軍功爵賞，皆決於外，歸而奏之。』
sentences.literal: military merit, noble ranks, and rewards were all decided outside and only then reported on return.'
sentences.idiomatic: promotions, titles, and bonuses were decided in the field and reported afterward.'

sentences.chinese: 此非空言也。
sentences.literal: This was not empty talk.
sentences.idiomatic: That was not hollow rhetoric.

sentences.chinese: 臣大父言李牧之為趙將居邊，軍市之租皆自用饗士，賞賜決於外，不從中復也。
sentences.literal: My grandfather said that when Li Mu was Zhao's general holding the frontier, the levies from the army market he used entirely himself to feast the troops; rewards and gifts were decided outside and were not re-checked from the center.
sentences.idiomatic: My grandfather told me how Li Mu, as Zhao's frontier commander, spent every coin of the army-market tax to feed his men and handed out rewards on his own authority, without waiting for approval from Xianyang.

sentences.chinese: 委任而責成功，故李牧乃得盡其知能，選車千三百乘，彀騎萬三千匹，百金之士十萬，是以北逐單于，破東胡，滅澹林，西抑強秦，南支韓、魏。
sentences.literal: He was entrusted with responsibility and held to success; therefore Li Mu was able to use all his knowledge and ability—he selected thirteen hundred war chariots, thirteen thousand mounted archers, a hundred thousand men worth a hundred in gold—and so drove the Chanyu northward, broke the Eastern Hu, destroyed the Danlin, checked mighty Qin to the west, and propped up Han and Wei to the south.
sentences.idiomatic: Given full trust and answerable only for results, Li Mu could deploy his genius: thirteen hundred chariots, thirteen thousand horse archers, and a hundred thousand picked warriors—enough to chase the Chanyu, crush the Eastern Hu, wipe out the Danlin, hold Qin at bay, and shield Han and Wei on the south.

sentences.chinese: 當是時，趙幾伯。
sentences.literal: At that time Zhao was almost hegemon among the states.
sentences.idiomatic: For a time Zhao nearly ranked as first among the powers.

sentences.chinese: 後會趙王遷立，其母倡也，用郭開讒，而誅李牧，令顏聚代之。
sentences.literal: Later, when King Qian of Zhao came to the throne—his mother had been a singing-girl—she employed Guo Kai's slander, executed Li Mu, and ordered Yan Ju to replace him.
sentences.idiomatic: Then King Qian of Zhao ascended; his mother was a former singing girl who listened to Guo Kai, had Li Mu put to death, and put Yan Ju in his place.

sentences.chinese: 是以為秦所滅。
sentences.literal: Therefore Zhao was destroyed by Qin.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhao fell to Qin because of it.

sentences.chinese: 今臣竊聞魏尚為雲中守，軍市租盡以給士卒，出私養錢，五日壹殺牛，以饗賓客軍吏舍人，是以匈奴遠避，不近雲中之塞。
sentences.literal: Now I have privately heard that Wei Shang as governor of Yunzhong spent all the army-market tax to supply the soldiers, paid out his own money, slaughtered an ox every five days to feast guests, officers, and retainers—therefore the Xiongnu kept their distance and did not approach the Yunzhong frontier.
sentences.idiomatic: I hear that Wei Shang, as governor of Yunzhong, poured every penny of the army-market revenue into his troops, dug into his own purse, and butchered a steer every five days for his staff—so the Xiongnu gave the Yunzhong walls a wide berth.

sentences.chinese: 虜嘗一入，尚帥車騎擊之，所殺甚眾。
sentences.literal: When the raiders once entered, Shang led chariots and cavalry against them and killed very many.
sentences.idiomatic: On the one raid they tried, Shang led horse and chariot against them and piled up a heavy butcher's bill.

sentences.chinese: 夫士卒盡家人子，起田中從軍，安知尺籍伍符？
sentences.literal: Now the rank and file are mostly sons of families, raised in the fields and drafted into the army—how should they know tally-rosters and five-man squad tallies?
sentences.idiomatic: These soldiers are farm boys called up from the plough; how are they to master every line of the regulations and squad tallies?

sentences.chinese: 終日力戰，斬首捕虜，上功莫府，一言不相應，文吏以法繩之。
sentences.literal: They fight with all their strength all day, behead enemies and take captives, report merit to the commandant's headquarters—if one word fails to match, the clerkly officials bind them with the statutes.
sentences.idiomatic: They spend the day in desperate combat, then send head-counts to headquarters; one slip in the paperwork and some desk-bound judge nails them to the letter of the law.

sentences.chinese: 其賞不行，吏奉法必用。
sentences.literal: Their rewards do not go through; the clerks invariably apply the law to the full.
sentences.idiomatic: Their battle pay never arrives, while the law officers enforce every comma.

sentences.chinese: 愚以為陛下法太明，賞太輕，罰太重。
sentences.literal: In my foolish view Your Majesty's laws are too clear, the rewards too slight, the punishments too heavy.
sentences.idiomatic: Forgive my bluntness, but I believe your code is too rigid, your rewards too stingy, and your penalties too harsh.

sentences.chinese: 且雲中守尚坐上功首虜差六級，陛下下之吏，削其爵，罰作之。
sentences.literal: Moreover the governor of Yunzhong, Shang, sat in judgment for overstating his beheaded captives by six grades; Your Majesty handed him to the clerks, stripped his rank, and sentenced him to labor.
sentences.idiomatic: Take Wei Shang: he overstated six heads in his battle report; you sent him to the judges, stripped his nobility, and set him to hard labor.

sentences.chinese: 繇此言之，陛下雖得李牧，不能用也。
sentences.literal: From this it follows that though Your Majesty obtained Li Mu, you could not employ him.
sentences.idiomatic: Put that together and even Li Mu could not have served you under such rules.

sentences.chinese: 臣誠愚，觸忌諱，死罪！」
sentences.literal: I am truly foolish; I have touched on forbidden matters—crime deserving death!"
sentences.idiomatic: I have been a fool and broached what I should not—worthy of death!"

sentences.chinese: 文帝說。
sentences.literal: Emperor Wen was pleased.
sentences.idiomatic: Emperor Wen's anger melted into approval.

sentences.chinese: 是日，令唐持節赦魏尚，復以為雲中守，而拜唐為車騎都尉，主中尉及郡國車士。
sentences.literal: That same day he ordered Tang to take the staff of authority and pardon Wei Shang, restoring him as governor of Yunzhong, and appointed Tang chief commandant of chariots and cavalry, in charge of the commandant of the capital and chariot troops of the commanderies and kingdoms.
sentences.idiomatic: That very day he sent Feng Tang with the imperial baton to free Wei Shang and reinstate him at Yunzhong, while Feng Tang himself was made chief commandant of chariots and cavalry, overseeing the metropolitan commandant and mounted forces from the provinces and principalities.

sentences.chinese: 汲黯字長孺，濮陽人也。
sentences.literal: Ji An, courtesy name Zhangru, was a native of Puyang.
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An, whose courtesy name was Zhangru, came from Puyang.

sentences.chinese: 其行有寵於古之衛君也。
sentences.literal: His conduct had favor with the ancient lord of Wei.
sentences.idiomatic: In his bearing he resembled the old lords of Wei who won the people's love.

sentences.chinese: 至黯十世，世為卿大夫。
sentences.literal: Down to An, for ten generations they had been ministers and grandees.
sentences.idiomatic: For ten generations before him the family had filled ministerial posts.

sentences.chinese: 以父任，孝景時為太子洗馬，以嚴見憚。
sentences.literal: By his father's privilege, under Emperor Jing he became groom of the heir apparent and was feared for his severity.
sentences.idiomatic: Recommended on his father's record, he served Emperor Jing as groom to the crown prince and was dreaded for his uncompromising sternness.

sentences.chinese: 武帝即位，黯為謁者。
sentences.literal: When Emperor Wu took the throne, An became an usher.
sentences.idiomatic: When Wu succeeded, Ji An was appointed an usher.

sentences.chinese: 東粵相攻，上使黯往視之。
sentences.literal: The Eastern Yue states attacked one another; the sovereign sent An to inspect.
sentences.idiomatic: When the Eastern Yue principalities turned on each other, the emperor dispatched Ji An to look into it.

sentences.chinese: 至吳而還，報曰：「粵人相攻，固其俗，不足以辱天子使者。」
sentences.literal: He turned back at Wu and reported, "The Yue people fighting one another is their custom; it is not worth troubling an envoy of the Son of Heaven."
sentences.idiomatic: He got only as far as Wu and sent word back: "Let the Yue fight among themselves—it is their habit and hardly merits an imperial envoy."

sentences.chinese: 河內失火，燒千餘家，上使黯往視之。
sentences.literal: A fire in Henei burned more than a thousand households; the sovereign sent An to inspect.
sentences.idiomatic: When fire ravaged a thousand homes in Henei, the emperor sent him again.

sentences.chinese: 還報曰：「家人失火，屋比延燒，不足憂。
sentences.literal: He returned and reported, "A common household fire spread along adjoining roofs; there is nothing to worry about.
sentences.idiomatic: His report read: "It was an ordinary house fire that jumped roof to roof—nothing for the court to fret over."

sentences.chinese: 臣過河內，河內貧人傷水旱萬餘家，或父子相食，臣謹以便宜，持節發河內倉粟以振貧民。
sentences.literal: Passing through Henei I saw more than ten thousand poor harmed by flood and drought, some fathers and sons eating one another; I dared, as expedient, to take the staff and open the Henei granaries to relieve the poor.
sentences.idiomatic: But on my way through Henei I found tens of thousands starving from flood and drought, some families reduced to cannibalism; I used my commission to open the government granaries and feed them.

sentences.chinese: 請歸節，伏矯制罰。」
sentences.literal: I beg to return the staff and submit to punishment for counterfeiting an order."
sentences.idiomatic: I now return the baton and ask to be punished for forging an imperial order.

sentences.chinese: 上賢而釋之，遷為滎陽令。
sentences.literal: The sovereign thought him worthy and released him, promoting him to magistrate of Xingyang.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor judged him loyal, forgave the offense, and named him magistrate of Xingyang.

sentences.chinese: 黯恥為令，稱疾歸田裡。
sentences.literal: An was ashamed to be a magistrate and pleaded illness, returning to his village fields.
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An found a county post beneath him, feigned illness, and went home to his fields.

sentences.chinese: 上聞，乃召為中大夫。
sentences.literal: When the sovereign heard of it, he summoned him to be grand counsellor of the palace.
sentences.idiomatic: When word reached the throne, Wu recalled him as grand counsellor of the palace.

sentences.chinese: 以數切諫，不得久留內，遷為東海太守。
sentences.literal: Because he remonstrated sharply and repeatedly, he could not long remain inside and was transferred to governor of the Eastern Sea.
sentences.idiomatic: His blunt memorials made him unwelcome at court, so he was shifted to governor of Donghai.

sentences.chinese: 黯學黃、老言，治官民，好清靜，擇丞史任之，責大指而已，不細苛。
sentences.literal: An studied Huang-Lao teaching; in governing officials and commoners he favored quietude, chose assistants and clerks to carry the burden, and insisted only on the main points, not petty harshness.
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An followed Huang-Lao principles: he ran his command with a light hand, delegated to capable deputies, and cared about broad outcomes rather than petty rules.

sentences.chinese: 黯多病，臥閣內不出。
sentences.literal: An was often ill and lay in his quarters without coming out.
sentences.idiomatic: Plagued by illness, he rarely left his inner office.

sentences.chinese: 歲餘，東海大治，稱之。
sentences.literal: After more than a year the Eastern Sea was greatly well ordered and he was praised.
sentences.idiomatic: Within a year Donghai was models of order and the people sang his praise.

sentences.chinese: 上聞，召為主爵都尉，列於九卿。
sentences.literal: When the sovereign heard of it, he summoned him to be chief commandant of chief rank for nobles, ranking with the nine ministers.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor then brought him back as chief commandant for noble ranks—one of the nine high ministers.

sentences.chinese: 治務在無為而已，引大體，不拘文法。
sentences.literal: His administration aimed at non-action; he cited the larger pattern and was not bound by written formulas.
sentences.idiomatic: His style was wuwei in the best sense: he set the tone and let the law follow common sense.

sentences.chinese: 為人性倨，少禮，面折，不能容人之過。
sentences.literal: By nature he was proud, short on ceremony, confronted others to their face, and could not tolerate others' faults.
sentences.idiomatic: He was haughty, scanted ritual, spoke his mind to men's faces, and brooked no foolishness.

sentences.chinese: 合己者善待之，不合者弗能忍見，士亦以此不附焉。
sentences.literal: Those who suited him he treated well; those who did not he could not bear to see—gentlemen therefore did not flock to him.
sentences.idiomatic: He warmed to allies and could hardly stand anyone else, so men of talent kept their distance.

sentences.chinese: 然好遊俠，任氣節，行修潔。
sentences.literal: Yet he loved wandering knights, trusted to spirit and integrity, and conducted himself with clean discipline.
sentences.idiomatic: Yet he admired knights-errant, honored personal honor, and kept his own hands clean.

sentences.chinese: 其諫，犯主之顏色。
sentences.literal: In remonstrance he offended the ruler's expression.
sentences.idiomatic: When he remonstrated he looked the emperor in the eye and contradicted him.

sentences.chinese: 常慕傅伯、爰盎之為人。
sentences.literal: He always admired the conduct of Fu Bo and Yuan Ang.
sentences.idiomatic: He modeled himself on Fu Bo and Yuan Ang.

sentences.chinese: 善灌夫、鄭當時及宗正劉棄疾。
sentences.literal: He was on good terms with Guan Fu, Zheng Dangshi, and Liu Qiji, director of the imperial clan.
sentences.idiomatic: He counted Guan Fu, Zheng Dangshi, and the director of the imperial clan Liu Qiji among his friends.

sentences.chinese: 亦以數直諫，不得久居位。
sentences.literal: Also because he remonstrated straight repeatedly, he could not long hold office.
sentences.idiomatic: His habit of blunt truth-telling kept him from staying long in any post.

sentences.chinese: 是時，太后弟武安侯田□分為丞相，中二千石拜謁，□分弗為禮。
sentences.literal: At that time the empress dowager's younger brother, Marquis of Wu'an Tian Fen, was chancellor; officials at two thousand piculs paid their respects, yet Fen did not return the courtesy.
sentences.idiomatic: Meanwhile Tian Fen, the empress dowager's brother and Marquis of Wu'an, sat as chancellor; ministers at the two-thousand-picul rank bowed to him, and he did not bother to bow back.

sentences.chinese: 黯見□分，未嘗拜，揖之。
sentences.literal: When An saw Fen he never bowed but merely inclined.
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An met him with a shallow bow, never a full prostration.

sentences.chinese: 上方招文學儒者，上曰吾欲云云，默對曰：「陛下內多欲而外施仁義，奈何欲效唐、虞之治乎！」
sentences.literal: The sovereign was recruiting men of letters and Confucians; the sovereign said he intended such-and-such; silently [An] replied, "Within, Your Majesty is full of desires, yet outwardly you dispense benevolence and righteousness—how then do you hope to imitate the government of Tang and Yu!"
sentences.idiomatic: While the court was packing itself with literati and the emperor aired his lofty plans, Ji An answered flatly: "You are a cauldron of appetites inside while preaching benevolence and righteousness to the world—how do you expect to match the age of Yao and Shun?"

sentences.chinese: 上怒，變色而罷朝。
sentences.literal: The emperor grew angry, changed color, and dismissed court.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor's face darkened; he cut court short and stalked off.

sentences.chinese: 公卿皆為黯懼。
sentences.literal: All the dukes and ministers feared for An.
sentences.idiomatic: The high ministers trembled for Ji An's safety.

sentences.chinese: 上退，謂人曰：「甚矣，汲黯之戇心！」
sentences.literal: When the sovereign withdrew he told someone, "Extreme indeed is Ji An's blunt stubbornness!"
sentences.idiomatic: Later Wu muttered to an attendant, "Ji An's obstinacy is becoming intolerable!"

sentences.chinese: 群臣或數黯，黯曰：「天子置公卿輔弼之臣，寧令從諛承意，陷主於不誼乎？
sentences.literal: Some of the ministers rebuked An; An said, "The Son of Heaven sets up dukes and ministers as supporting ministers—is it to make them flatter and carry out his wishes, trapping the ruler in what is not right?
sentences.idiomatic: When colleagues scolded him, Ji An shot back: "The throne appoints us as his brace and bracket—are we here to flatter him into moral disaster?

sentences.chinese: 且已在其位，縱愛身，奈辱朝廷何！」
sentences.literal: Moreover, once one holds the post, even if one cherishes one's person, how can one bear to disgrace the court!"
sentences.idiomatic: And once you accept the seal, clinging to your skin while the dynasty is shamed is the greater cowardice!

sentences.chinese: 黯多病，病且滿三月，上常賜告者數，終不愈。
sentences.literal: An was often ill; his illness neared three months; the sovereign repeatedly granted him leave to recover, yet he never got better.
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An was chronically sick; as three months neared, the emperor kept extending his sick leave, but he did not mend.

sentences.chinese: 最後，嚴助為請告。
sentences.literal: Finally Yan Zhu spoke for him requesting leave.
sentences.idiomatic: At last Yan Zhu begged the throne on his behalf.

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「汲黯何如人也？」
sentences.literal: The sovereign said, "What sort of man is Ji An?"
sentences.idiomatic: Wu asked, "What kind of man is Ji An really?"

sentences.chinese: 曰：「使黯任職居官，亡以愈人，然至其輔少主守成，雖自謂賁、育弗能奪也。」
sentences.literal: [Yan Zhu] said, "If you put An in office to handle affairs, he will not surpass others; yet when it comes to supporting a young ruler and guarding what is established, even men who call themselves Ben and Yu could not budge him."
sentences.idiomatic: Yan Zhu replied: "Set him to routine administration and he seems ordinary; but charge him with guarding a young heir and the legacy of the house—even Ben and Yu could not shake his resolve."

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「然。
sentences.literal: The sovereign said, "So.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor said, "True enough."

sentences.chinese: 古有社稷之臣，至如汲黯，近之矣！」
sentences.literal: In antiquity there were ministers for the altars of soil and grain; as for Ji An, he approaches that!"
sentences.idiomatic: The old histories speak of ministers who were the state itself—Ji An comes close to that ideal!

sentences.chinese: 大將軍青侍中，上踞廁視之。
sentences.literal: General-in-chief Wei Qing attended within; the sovereign squatted on the privy-stool to see him.
sentences.idiomatic: When General Wei Qing waited on him in private, the emperor would receive him from the privy seat, legs sprawled.

sentences.chinese: 丞相弘宴見，上或時不冠。
sentences.literal: When Chancellor Hong had informal audience, the sovereign sometimes did not wear the cap.
sentences.idiomatic: With Chancellor Gongsun Hong at a casual audience he sometimes went bareheaded.

sentences.chinese: 至如見黯，不冠不見也。
sentences.literal: But as for seeing An, without the cap he would not meet him.
sentences.idiomatic: For Ji An he always donned full court dress.

sentences.chinese: 上嘗坐武帳，黯前奏事，上不冠，望見黯，避帷中，使人可其奏。
sentences.literal: The sovereign once sat in the military tent; An came forward to memorialize; the sovereign was not capped; seeing An from afar he hid behind the curtain and had someone approve his memorial.
sentences.idiomatic: Once, seated in the war tent without his crown, he spotted Ji An approaching and slipped behind the screen, letting an attendant approve the memorial for him.

sentences.chinese: 其見敬禮如此。
sentences.literal: Thus was he respected and treated with ceremony.
sentences.idiomatic: Such was the deference the emperor showed him.

sentences.chinese: 張湯以更定律令為廷尉，黯質責湯於上前，曰：「公為正卿，上不能褒先帝之功業，下不能化天下之邪心，安國富民，使囹圄空虛，何空取高皇帝約束紛更之為？
sentences.literal: Zhang Tang, having revised statutes and ordinances, became commandant of justice; An confronted and rebuked Tang before the sovereign, saying, "You are chief minister; above you cannot extol the late emperor's achievements, below you cannot transform the wicked hearts of the world, settle the state and enrich the people, or empty the prisons—why do you vainly take Gaozu's established rules and alter them in confusion?
sentences.idiomatic: When Zhang Tang rewrote the code and became commandant of justice, Ji An cornered him in open court: "As chief minister you neither celebrate the founder's legacy nor reform public morals, bring peace to the realm, fill the granaries, nor clear the jails—why do you busy yourself tearing apart the statutes Gaozu left behind?

sentences.chinese: 而公以此無種矣！」
sentences.literal: And for this you will leave no posterity!"
sentences.idiomatic: For that you will die without descendants!

sentences.chinese: 黯時與湯論議，湯辯常在文深小苛，黯憤發，罵曰：「天下謂刀筆吏不可為公卿，果然。
sentences.literal: When An debated policy with Tang, Tang's argumentation always lay in twisted wording and petty harshness; An flared with anger and cursed, saying, "The world says brush-and-knife clerks must not become dukes and ministers—it is indeed so.
sentences.idiomatic: In policy debates Zhang Tang always hid behind legal hair-splitting; Ji An lost his temper and swore, "They say a clerk should never rise to high office—how true.

sentences.chinese: 必湯也，令天下重足而立，仄目而視矣！」
sentences.literal: If it must be Tang, he will make all under heaven stand with feet together and look sideways!"
sentences.idiomatic: Let men like Zhang Tang win, and the whole empire will walk on tiptoe and glance sideways in fear!

sentences.chinese: 是時，漢方征匈奴，招懷四夷。
sentences.literal: At that time Han was just campaigning against the Xiongnu and winning over the four Yi.
sentences.idiomatic: The Han was then deep in the Xiongnu wars and courting the border tribes.

sentences.chinese: 黯務少事，間常言與胡和親，毋起兵。
sentences.literal: An strove for few affairs and often spoke of peace and kinship with the Hu, not raising troops.
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An pushed for less government and urged marriage alliances with the steppe instead of new campaigns.

sentences.chinese: 上方鄉儒術，尊公孫弘，及事益多，吏民巧。
sentences.literal: The sovereign was turning toward Confucian learning, honored Gongsun Hong, and as affairs grew more numerous, clerks and commoners grew crafty.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor meanwhile embraced Confucianism, elevated Gongsun Hong, and as business multiplied, officials and people grew expert at evasion.

sentences.chinese: 上分別文法，湯等數奏決讞以幸。
sentences.literal: The sovereign distinguished textual niceties in the law; Tang and others repeatedly memorialized verdicts to win favor.
sentences.idiomatic: Wu delighted in legal exegesis; Zhang Tang and his circle fed him case after case to curry favor.

sentences.chinese: 而黯常毀儒，面觸弘等徒懷詐飾智以阿人主取容，而刀筆之吏專深文巧詆，陷人於罔，以自為功。
sentences.literal: But An often disparaged Confucians; to the face he attacked Hong and the rest for harboring deceit and displaying cleverness to fawn on the ruler and win accommodation, while brush-and-knife clerks specialized in twisted texts and clever slanders, trapping people in nets to make their own merit.
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An, meanwhile, trashed the Ru scholars and accused Hong to his face of faking wisdom to please the throne, while clerks twisted the code to frame the innocent and pad their résumés.

sentences.chinese: 上愈益貴弘、湯，弘、湯心疾黯，雖上亦不說也，欲誅之以事。
sentences.literal: The sovereign came more and more to esteem Hong and Tang; Hong and Tang inwardly hated An—though the sovereign too was displeased—and wished to execute him on some pretext.
sentences.idiomatic: Wu prized Hong and Tang all the more; they loathed Ji An, and the emperor himself cooled—yet all three looked for a charge that would let them kill him.

sentences.chinese: 弘為丞相，乃言上曰：「右內史界部中多貴人宗室，難治，非素重臣弗能任，請徙黯。」
sentences.literal: Hong being chancellor, then spoke to the sovereign, saying, "The territory of the right metropolitan superintendent has many nobles and imperial clansmen; it is hard to govern; unless it is a minister of long-standing weight he cannot bear the burden—I beg to transfer An."
sentences.idiomatic: As chancellor, Gongsun Hong told the emperor that the right capital district swarmed with nobles and imperial kin, needed an iron hand, and ought to be given to Ji An.

sentences.chinese: 為右內史數歲，官事不廢。
sentences.literal: As right metropolitan superintendent for several years, official business was not neglected.
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An held the post for years without letting administration slide.

sentences.chinese: 大將軍青既益尊，姊為皇后，然黯與亢禮。
sentences.literal: After General-in-chief Qing grew more honored and his elder sister became empress, yet An treated him with the etiquette of equals.
sentences.idiomatic: Even after Wei Qing's star rose and his sister became empress, Ji An refused to grovel.

sentences.chinese: 或說黯曰：「自天子欲令群臣下大將軍，大將軍尊貴，誠重，君不可以不拜。」
sentences.literal: Someone urged An, saying, "The Son of Heaven wishes the ministers to rank below the general-in-chief; the general-in-chief is honored and weighty—in truth you cannot but bow."
sentences.idiomatic: Friends warned him: "The court wants everyone below the general-in-chief; his rank is real—you really should prostrate yourself."

sentences.chinese: 黯曰：「夫以大將軍有揖客，反不重耶？」
sentences.literal: An said, "That the general-in-chief has retainers who receive only an incline—does that not show his weight?"
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An retorted: "If the general-in-chief accepts a bow from a man who only inclines, does that not prove his stature?"

sentences.chinese: 大將軍聞，愈賢黯，數請問以朝廷所疑，遇黯加於平日。
sentences.literal: When the general-in-chief heard, he thought An more worthy, repeatedly asked him about doubts in court policy, and treated An with greater regard than in ordinary times.
sentences.idiomatic: Wei Qing heard this, respected him the more, and sought his counsel on state questions, honoring him above his usual circle.

sentences.chinese: 淮南王謀反，憚黯，曰：「黯好直諫，守節死義；
sentences.literal: The king of Huainan plotted rebellion and feared An, saying, "An loves straight remonstrance and guards integrity unto death;
sentences.idiomatic: The king of Huainan, plotting revolt, named Ji An as a danger: "He lives for blunt remonstrance and would die before betraying principle;

sentences.chinese: 至說公孫弘等，如發蒙耳。」
sentences.literal: as for persuading Gongsun Hong and the rest, it would be like lifting a blind."
sentences.idiomatic: winning over men like Gongsun Hong would be child's play by comparison.

sentences.chinese: 上既數征匈奴有功，黯言益不用。
sentences.literal: Once the sovereign had repeatedly scored successes against the Xiongnu, An's words were less heeded.
sentences.idiomatic: After the northern campaigns began to succeed, nobody listened to Ji An's protests.

sentences.chinese: 始黯列九卿矣，而公孫弘、張湯為小吏。
sentences.literal: At first An ranked with the nine ministers while Gongsun Hong and Zhang Tang were petty clerks.
sentences.idiomatic: Ji An had been a nine-minister man when Hong and Tang were still clerks.

sentences.chinese: 及弘、湯稍貴，與黯同位，黯又非毀弘、湯。
sentences.literal: When Hong and Tang gradually grew noble and shared rank with An, An again condemned and disparaged Hong and Tang.
sentences.idiomatic: When they rose to his level, he still spoke of them with contempt.

sentences.chinese: 已而弘至丞相，封侯，湯御史大夫，黯時丞史皆與同列，或尊用過之。
sentences.literal: In time Hong reached chancellor and was enfeoffed as marquis; Tang became imperial counsellor; at that time An's assistants and clerks were of the same rank, some honored and employed above him.
sentences.idiomatic: Soon Hong was chancellor and a marquis, Tang imperial counsellor, while Ji An's old subordinates passed him in rank.

sentences.chinese: 黯褊心，不能無少望，見上，言曰：「陛下用群臣如積薪耳，後來者居上。」
sentences.literal: An's heart was narrow; he could not but harbor some resentment; seeing the sovereign he said, "Your Majesty employs the ministers like stacking firewood—the later arrivals sit on top."
sentences.idiomatic: Bitterness ate at him; he told the emperor, "You stack your ministers like firewood—the last logs tossed on end up on top."

sentences.chinese: 黯罷，上曰：「人果不可以無學，觀汲黯之言，日益甚矣。」
sentences.literal: When An withdrew the sovereign said, "Indeed a man cannot lack learning; observe Ji An's words—they grow daily more extreme!"
sentences.idiomatic: After Ji An left, Wu remarked, "A man really must read his books—listen to Ji An now; he grows cruder by the day."

sentences.chinese: 居無何，匈奴渾邪王帥眾來降，漢發車二萬乘。
sentences.literal: Before long the Xiongnu Hunye king led his people to surrender; Han requisitioned twenty thousand chariots.
sentences.idiomatic: Not long after, the Hunye king came in with his tribes and the court ordered twenty thousand wagons to meet him.

sentences.chinese: 縣官亡錢，從民貰馬。
sentences.literal: The treasury had no money and borrowed horses from the people.
sentences.idiomatic: The exchequer was empty, so the government borrowed horses from households.

sentences.chinese: 民或匿馬，馬不具。
sentences.literal: Some of the people hid their horses and the horses were not complete.
sentences.idiomatic: Citizens hid their animals and the convoy could not be filled.

sentences.chinese: 上怒，欲斬長安令。
sentences.literal: The emperor grew angry and wished to execute the magistrate of Chang'an.
sentences.idiomatic: In a fury the emperor threatened to behead the magistrate of Chang'an.
